I have been relatively quiet this week in the midst of such volatility and with very good reason because I am on a break in China (my very maiden trip here).

What I have seen and experienced so far related to energy:

1️⃣ High speed train reaching a speed of 348 km/h from Beijing to Tianjin, electric powered, no less

2️⃣ Busy roads and highways, quieter than most because of influx of EV vehicles. But it doesn't mean no traditional transport fuelled cars though.

3️⃣ Lots of English words everywhere but not much spoken (good luck with ordering food in a random shop!)

4️⃣ Amazing skyscrapers and construction in many places. Tianjin in particular surprised me with vibrant lights and laser show on the facade of multiple buildings 

5️⃣ A strong sense of culture steeped in thousands of years of history yet striving for innovation and hunger for success

Can't say I have witnessed demand destruction first hand.

Photo taken at Temple of Heaven where I heard some Americans talking to a local about Elon Musk being there recently. I was thinking of Trump more as I walked up those steps!

text:
U.S. Petroleum Depletion Imposed Deadline on Hormuz War

Since the start of the war with Iran, depletion of the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) has bought time for the U.S. government to develop both diplomatic and military options to re-open the Strait of Hormuz. 

The United States has explored diplomatic negotiations via Pakistan and other mediators, as well as attempts to unblock the Strait by force, organising via virtual convoys using aircraft and drones to protect tankers in the waterway. 

Some tankers carrying crude, liquefied natural gas (LNG) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) have successfully transited as a result of diplomatic agreements or under the armed protection of the United States.  

Since announcing a ceasefire in early April, the United States has pursued a mixed strategy of bombing-while-negotiating, holding talks while trying to increase the number of transits under cover of darkness. 

The intention appears to have been to pressure Iran into accepting a deal while at the same time degrading Iran’s ability to threaten shipping in case the talks fail. 

But with petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers have faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks from trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

INVESTMENT MANAGERS have cut their bullish net long position in European gas futures and options to the lowest for three months in expectation the ceasefire between the United States and Iran will allow regular LNG exports through the Strait of Hormuz to resume and make refilling storage easier and cheaper. Fund managers sold Dutch TTF futures options for the eighth time in the last eleven weeks. The combined position had been cut to 235 terawatt-hours on June 12 down from a high of 323 TWh on March 27:

Just realized I made a massive fat-finger error on the EUR/USD levels in my last post. A buddy of mine who’s riding the same Euro short called me out on it. I typed 1.35–1.4, which was a complete typo for 1.135–1.14.

There I was, giving him a whole breakdown on why the ECB is bound to crush the Euro, completely oblivious to the fact that I was talking straight gibberish because of a typo in the original print.

He must've been scratching his head thinking, 'Why the hell is he talking down the Euro with a 1.35–1.4 target?' Sorry to anyone else on the timeline who got thrown off by that. It was definitely meant to be 1.135–1.14. My bad.

South Africa welcomes the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding between the United States of America and the Islamic Republic of Iran
18 June 2026

South Africa welcomes the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between the United States of America and the Islamic Republic of Iran, declaring the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon, as well as the undertaking not to initiate any war or any military operation and to refrain from the threat or use of force against each other.

South Arica urges all regional actors to respect the de-escalation of military conflict and work towards the full implementation of the MoU and the conclusion of a final agreement.

Welcoming the MoU, Minister of International Relations and Cooperation, Mr Ronald Lamola, said: “The agreement between the United States and Iran hopefully ends the unlawful use of force and violations of the UN Charter in this war. South Africa has consistently called for international disputes to be settled by negotiation – not war. All countries particularly developing countries have felt the economic repercussions of this war. This MoU thus provides the framework for durable peace and regional stability in the Middle East as it reaffirms the need for peace and the respect of the sovereignty and territorial integrity of all States.”

Consistent with South Africa’s view, the MoU provides an opportunity to address all outstanding areas within the framework of multilateralism, through the International Atomic Energy Agency and the United Nations Security Council.
The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is critical to stabilise the volatility in global oil markets and to alleviate the energy and food security challenges faced by developing countries in order to reverse its adverse impact on growth and recovery trajectories.

Furthermore, the undertaking to terminate all types of sanctions against Iran is also welcomed as it will bring about relief to the Iranian people following decades of hardship.

South Africa extends its appreciation to the Government of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, together with the Sultanate of Oman and the State of Qatar, including other countries who played a positive role in the process, for their dedicated and steadfast commitment to dialogue and constructive engagement.
We recall the words of our late President Nelson Mandela: “No problem is so deep that it cannot be overcome, given the will of all parties, through discussion and negotiation rather than force and violence.”

South Africa encourages all parties engaged in this process to work towards achieving a just and lasting peace, stability and development in the Middle East and beyond.

ISSUED BY THE DEPARTMENT OF INTERNATIONAL RELATIONS AND COOPERATION

OR Tambo Building 460 Soutpansberg Road Rietondale Pretoria 0084

#US #CrudeOil #Inventories 🛢️⛽️

📍US commercial crude oil inventories are reaching critical levels:

Crude inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the largest commercial storage hub in the US and the pricing point for WTI Crude, dropped -1.6M 🛢️ last week, to 20M 🛢️, (..) https://t.co/MY2ylY7YUP

Cushing, Oklahoma crude inventories have fallen to their lowest level since October 2014 according to the latest data.

Eight consecutive weekly draws have pushed stocks just above the roughly 20 million barrel level widely viewed as the hub’s functional “tank bottom.” Cushing is a key U.S. oil storage hub and serves as the physical settlement point for West Texas Intermediate futures.

While total capacity is near 90 million barrels, headline storage figures may overstate usable supply, as a portion of remaining crude can include sludge or material that is difficult to move operationally.

The drawdown is part of a broader decline in U.S. crude inventories. The Department of Energy report showed a national crude drawdown of 8.26 million barrels, compared with a consensus estimate of 3.5 million barrels.

Source: The Deep Dive

#OilMarkets #CrudeOil #WTI #Cushing #EnergyMarkets #Commodities #OilStorage #USOil #EnergyNews #MacroMarkets #EIA #CrudeInventories #MarketUpdate #OilAndGas #SupplyChain

Cushing crude inventories have collapsed to 20 million barrels, the lowest since 2014, after 8 consecutive weekly declines totaling 8.3 million barrels. Storage is now below 2 days of US production, approaching critical operational thresholds where extraction becomes difficult and costly.

$BTC #BTC #MACRO #ENERGY

US commercial crude oil inventories are reaching critical levels:

Crude inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the largest commercial storage hub in the US and the pricing point for WTI Crude, dropped -1.6 million barrels last week, to 20 million barrels, the lowest since 2014.

This marks the 8th consecutive weekly decline, totaling -8.3 million barrels.

As a result, Cushing now holds less than 2 days worth of US crude production, approaching the minimum level at which the facility can continue pumping oil efficiently.

Once inventories fall below ~20 million barrels, extracting crude becomes technically difficult and more costly, while oil quality can deteriorate due to water and sediment.

Meanwhile, the Strategic Petroleum Reserve is down to ~340 million barrels, the lowest since 1983, after 172 million barrels were released to contain war-driven fuel price increases.

US oil inventories down to levels rarely seen in modern energy markets.

PADD 2 is getting sucked dry as Gulf pulls barrels to export. Makes sense considering that PADD 2 has Canadian heavy sour barrels that are  in demand right now.

PADD 3 has SPR and enough stockpile to last through June.

But  what’s really worth highlighting here is the PADD 2 and 4, landlocked,  crude imports are falling. Land imports have dropped to 3.129 million  b/d, normally over 3.4 million b/d.

Cushing is already near tank  bottom, so the Gulf can no longer pull barrels from inland to export,  which means the incoming crude draws will be mostly in PADD 3. Canada  doesn’t have enough barrels to export to the US now so flows will remain  low.

As PADD 3 crude inventories drop and as SPR flow rate  slows, US crude exports will inevitably drop. This will force oversea  markets to tighten.

🚢 “Panuca” Oil Tanker on the Houston Ship Canal — Texas at the Heart of Global Energy

In the mid-20th century, the Houston Ship Canal transformed local industry into global influence, and vessels like the oil tanker Panuca were daily evidence of that shift. Built to connect Houston’s industrial core with the Gulf of Mexico, the Ship Canal — dredged and expanded for oceangoing traffic — became a vital artery for energy exports, agricultural goods, and global commerce.

The Panuca, owned by Sinclair-Gulf Refining Co., represents a class of “well-known vessels” that carried petroleum products from Texas refineries to ports around the world. These tankers were more than transport machines; they were floating links in a supply chain that shaped geopolitics, naval strategy, and industrial growth from the Gulf Coast to Europe and beyond.

Houston’s rise as an energy capital depended on this maritime network. As refineries multiplied along the Ship Canal, so did the jobs, infrastructure, and urban expansion that turned Houston into one of America’s largest cities. Looking at a tanker navigating those waters reminds us how Texas’s natural resources and technological ingenuity combined to create a global energy engine.

#TexasHistory #HoustonShipCanal #EnergyEconomy #MaritimeTrade 🚢🛢️

Let's clear up the confusion about "gas prices" together. Yes, they are high.. especially in CA. However, to really understand them, we have to talk about the "per barrel" price which is how they're traded.

When people talk about gas (oil) prices "per barrel," they're usually referring to crude oil — the raw stuff that gets refined into gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, etc.

Crude oil prices are set by global markets (supply, demand, wars, recessions, OPEC decisions, etc.), not by any one administration. They heavily influence what you pay at the pump, but not 100% (refining, distribution, taxes, and retailer margins also matter).

Here's a simple, factual look at West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil prices — the main U.S. benchmark — across recent administrations.

Data comes from sources like the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) and Federal Reserve economic data. I've focused on trends and specifically answered: How many days were prices above $100 per barrel?

Quick Overview by Administration:

Obama (Jan 2009 – Jan 2017) >$100 over 900 days
Prices started low after the 2008 crash, then rose with economic recovery and global events (like the Arab Spring and Libya issues). They stayed above $100 for much of 2011 through mid-2014 — that's over 3 years, roughly 900+ trading days above $100. Prices then dropped sharply in 2014–2016 as U.S. shale oil production surged and global supply increased.  Overall average: ~$70–$80

Trump 45 (Jan 2017 – Jan 2021)  >$100 0 days
Prices mostly stayed in the $40–$76 range. They never had any sustained time above $100 (essentially 0 days above $100 during the term; any marginal days were negligible or none). The big story was the COVID-19 crash in 2020, when demand collapsed and futures briefly went negative. At this time Trump filled all of the emergency reserves at the lowest price. Overall average: ~$50–$55

Biden (Jan 2021 – Jan 2025) >$100 over 130 days
Prices recovered after COVID lows. Then came the 2022 spike triggered by Russia's invasion of Ukraine (plus related supply concerns). Prices briefly topped $120 and stayed elevated for months. Roughly 130–150 days above $100 total — mostly concentrated in spring/summer 2022 (consistent stretch from March–June, plus some intermittent days after). Prices trended down later in the term to the $70–$80 range by the end. Overall average: ~$75–$80

Trump 47 (Jan 2025 – present) >$100 10-20 days
Early in the term, prices were relatively low (dipped below $60 in parts of early 2026). Then, in spring 2026, Middle East tensions (including conflict involving Iran and disruptions like the Strait of Hormuz) caused a sharp but brief spike. Prices crossed above $100 in late March 2026 (first time since 2022), peaked over $110 briefly in early April, then fell back quickly after de-escalation. This high period lasted only a short time — roughly 10–20 trading days above $100. As of mid-June 2026, prices are around $76 per barrel. Average so far: Likely in the $65–$75 range.

The Big Picture:

High oil prices are usually tied to global events (wars, pandemics, supply shocks) more than domestic policy alone.

The U.S. has been a top oil producer with record or near-record output in recent years under multiple administrations.

Prices go up and down under both parties. No administration "owns" the market.

What you pay at the pump lags crude prices and includes many other factors.

Oil markets are complex and volatile — that's why prices swing. Understanding the "per barrel" reality helps cut through the noise. If you're seeing wild claims online, check the actual daily/weekly data from EIA or FRED for yourself.

Stay informed, stay safe out there. What questions do you have about this? I'm happy to dig deeper.
